skill.name: iotnet
skill.description: Analyze IoT packet captures and live network traffic to identify protocols, plaintext data, weak authentication, and insecure device behavior.
skill.summary: Detect IoT protocols and network security issues in packet captures or live traffic.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell command examples and sudo-based live capture instructions. Review confirms these are documentation for an IoT network security tool, not hidden malware, but they can run external commands with user-provided paths, filters, and elevated privileges. Weak cryptography and hardcoded IP indicators appear to be false positives or examples; no prompt injection or exfiltration intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Live Packet Capture
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run live traffic capture with sudo. This is legitimate for packet capture, but it grants elevated privileges and may expose sensitive network data if used outside an authorized environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo examples and privilege note are explicit. The context is a security tool, so this is operationally risky but not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution With User Inputs
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the assistant to execute iotnet commands using file paths, interfaces, IP filters, capture filters, display filters, and config paths supplied during the workflow. This can be safe when arguments are validated and quoted, but it creates command execution and injection risk if used carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clear and depend on user-provided values. No direct malicious payload is present, but shell execution risk is real.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Private IP Example
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP finding is a documentation example for filtering traffic by one private network address. It does not identify an external destination or data exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The address is in an example command and uses a private RFC 1918 range. There is no evidence that traffic is sent to this address.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Scanner False Positive
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography on metadata and heading lines, but those lines contain the skill name and network analysis description. No cryptographic algorithm use was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not call or recommend weak algorithms. The scan result appears to be a keyword collision rather than a real cryptographic issue.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sudo Command Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes sudo commands for live capture. Users should approve the interface, duration, and filters before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in command examples. It is expected for live capture, but still requires explicit user authorization.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Reconnaissance Capability
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is designed to inspect IoT network traffic, identify protocols, and find insecure behavior. This is useful for authorized assessment and risky for unauthorized monitoring.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The capability is explicitly described. The file presents it as defensive analysis, with no evidence of stealth, persistence, or exfiltration.

content.value_statement: IoT devices often expose insecure protocols, plaintext credentials, and weak authentication in network traffic. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through PCAP and live capture analysis with iotnet.
actual_capabilities: Analyze one or more PCAP files with the iotnet command.
Capture live traffic from a named interface for a set duration.
Filter analysis by IP address, BPF capture filters, or display filters.
Identify IoT protocols such as MQTT, CoAP, Zigbee, Z-Wave, ONVIF, UPnP, SSDP, and Modbus.
Report security findings such as plaintext credentials, missing TLS, weak authentication, and insecure protocol versions.
Use custom IoT detection rules from a configuration file.
limitations: Live capture requires root or sudo privileges on the host.
The skill depends on the iotnet command being installed and accessible.
PCAP results depend on the traffic present in the capture file.
It does not replace manual validation of network ownership and authorization.

use_cases.title: Review Device Lab Traffic
use_cases.description: Analyze PCAP files from test devices to find plaintext protocols, weak authentication, and risky IoT behavior.

use_cases.title: Triage Field Network Captures
use_cases.description: Inspect captured traffic from a customer or site investigation and summarize IoT protocol exposure.

use_cases.title: Validate Product Security Testing
use_cases.description: Run targeted filters for MQTT, CoAP, or device IPs and document findings for remediation work.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze One Capture
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this IoT packet capture with iotnet: [path]. Summarize detected protocols, vulnerabilities, and recommended fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Filter One Device
prompt_templates.prompt: Use iotnet to analyze [pcap path] for device IP [address]. Focus on plaintext traffic, weak authentication, and unusual protocol use.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Live Traffic
prompt_templates.prompt: Capture authorized IoT traffic on interface [interface] for [duration] seconds with iotnet. Use filter [filter] and explain any sudo command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Use Custom Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze [pcap paths] with iotnet using custom rules at [config path]. Compare default findings with custom rule findings and prioritize remediation.

output_examples.input: Analyze a PCAP from a smart camera test network.
output: Detected ONVIF, SSDP, and HTTP traffic from the camera.
High risk: credentials appeared in plaintext HTTP requests.
Recommendation: require TLS, disable unused discovery services, and rotate exposed credentials.

output_examples.input: Review MQTT traffic for a single IoT gateway.
output: Detected MQTT traffic on the expected broker port.
Medium risk: messages were not encrypted in the capture.
Recommendation: enforce MQTT over TLS and verify broker authentication policy.

output_examples.input: Run a short authorized live capture on a lab interface.
output: Capture duration and interface were confirmed before execution.
Detected CoAP traffic and several multicast discovery packets.
Recommendation: restrict discovery traffic to the lab VLAN and review device authentication.

best_practices: Use this skill only on networks and packet captures you are authorized to inspect.
Confirm interface names, durations, filters, and sudo commands before running live capture.
Store PCAP files and findings securely because they may contain credentials or personal data.
anti_patterns: Do not run live capture on third-party networks without written authorization.
Do not pass untrusted filter strings or file paths into shell commands without validation.
Do not treat automated findings as final without reviewing packet context and device behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill require live network access?
faq.answer: No. It can analyze existing PCAP files without live network access.

faq.question: Why does live capture require sudo?
faq.answer: Most systems require elevated privileges to capture packets from a network interface.

faq.question: Can it detect MQTT and CoAP?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill describes detection for MQTT, CoAP, and other IoT protocols.

faq.question: Can I use custom detection rules?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports a custom configuration file for IoT detection rules.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it can run external commands and live packet capture.

faq.question: What should I check before using it?
faq.answer: Verify authorization, confirm the target interface or PCAP path, and protect any sensitive packet data.
